elements.sent-2411-002: Recent advancements in 3D generation models have opened new possibilities for simulating dynamic 3D object movements and customizing behaviors, yet creating this content remains challenging.
elements.sent-2411-003: Current methods often require manual assignment of precise physical properties for simulations or rely on video generation models to predict them, which is computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we rethink the usage of multi-modal large language model (MLLM) in physics-based simulation, and present \our, a physics-based approach that endows static 3D objects with interactive dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-005: We begin with detailed scene reconstruction and object-level 3D open-vocabulary segmentation, progressing to multi-view image in-painting.
elements.sent-2411-006: Inspired by human visual reasoning, we propose MLLM-based Physical Property Perception (MLLM-P3) to predict mean physical properties of objects in a zero-shot manner.
elements.sent-2411-007: Based on the mean values and the object's geometry, the Material Property Distribution Prediction model (MPDP) model then estimates the full distribution, reformulating the problem as probability distribution estimation to reduce computational costs.

elements.sent-2411-018: For example, PhysDreamer~\cite{zhang2024physdreamer employs stable video diffusion model to learn Young's modulus of objects.}

elements.sent-2411-023: We therefore ask this question: how can we develop models for perceiving physics from just visual data? To this end, we rethink physics-based simulation and the usage of multi-modal large language model (MLLM), such as GPT-4V~\cite{yang2023dawn .}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this paper, we propose \our, a novel physics-based method that transforms static 3D objects into interactive ones capable of responding to new interactions, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-025: We first segment objects in an open-word scene with priors from foundation models~\cite{liu2023grounding,kirillov2023segment,zhang2024recognize .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Inspired by how humans predict physical properties of objects through visual data, our \our leverages MLLM-based Physical Property Perception (MLLM-P3) to predict the mean values of physical properties.

elements.sent-2411-245: Inspired by prior successful works, we innovatively introduce the integration of 2D open-vocabulary detector models, such as Grounding DINO, promptable 2D segmentation models, such as SAM, image tagging models like RAM.
elements.sent-2411-246: The integrated 2D open-vocabulary model can automatically segment objects within images without the need for any textual input.
elements.sent-2411-247: Specifically, given an input image, we first employ an image tagging model, RAM to get the tags of the image.
elements.sent-2411-248: Then, given the tags, we employ Grounding DINO to generate precise boxes for objects or regions within the image by leveraging the textual information in tags as condition.
elements.sent-2411-249: Subsequently, the annotated boxes obtained through Grounding DINO serve as the box prompts for SAM to generate precise mask annotations.
elements.sent-2411-250: By leveraging the capabilities of these robust expert models, our method enables the automatic labeling of an entire image.. 3D Open-vocabulary Segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-251: After 2D open-vocabulary segmentation, the segmented images contain rich semantic features for every object in the 3D scene.
elements.sent-2411-252: We effectively lift these 2D masks to segment anything in the 3D scene via radiance fields rendering.
elements.sent-2411-253: Given a pre-trained 3D scene, inspired by recent works, we preserve all attributes of the Gaussians, but add a semantic attribute to integrate semantic information for each Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-254: Then, to assign each 2D mask a unique ID in the 3D scene, we need to associate the masks of the same identity across different views.
elements.sent-2411-255: We employ a well-trained zero-shot tracker to propagate and associate these masks.
elements.sent-2411-256: In addition to the existing Gaussian properties, we introduce a new parameter, semantic attribute, to each Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-257: The semantic attribute is a learnable and compact vector, which is used to distinguish semantic categories in whole 3D scene.
elements.sent-2411-258: To optimize the introduced attribute of each Gaussian, we render semantic attribute into 2D images in a differentiable manner as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{S} = \sum_{p \in \mathcal{N}} y_{p} \alpha_{p} \prod_{j=1}^{p-1}\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right),\end{equation} where $\mathcal{S _k$ represents the 2D semantic labels of pixel $k$, derived from Gaussian point semantic attributes via $\alpha$-blending.}
elements.sent-2411-259: Here, $y_p$ denotes the semantic attribute of the 3D Gaussian point $p$, and $\alpha_p$ is the influence factor of this point in rendering pixels.
elements.sent-2411-260: After associating 2D instance labels across each training view, we apply the grouping loss and 3D Gaussian reconstruction loss to supervise the optimization progress.

elements.section-2411-016: More Details about Material Point Method (MPM)
elements.sent-2411-283: The Material Point Method (MPM) is an advanced numerical technique for simulating the behavior of continuum materials.
elements.sent-2411-284: It discretizes a material body into material points, commonly referred to as particles, which carry essential properties such as mass, velocity, deformation gradient, and stress.
elements.sent-2411-285: These particles interact with a background computational grid, which facilitates spatial derivative calculations and the application of external forces.
elements.sent-2411-286: MPM consists of two primary phases: 1) Particle-to-Grid (P2G) Transfer: Particles transfer their properties to the grid, enabling the computation of global quantities such as forces and accelerations.
elements.sent-2411-287: This dual transfer mechanism allows MPM to efficiently handle large deformations and complex interactions in continuum materials.. Particle-to-Grid (P2G) Transfer.
elements.sent-2411-288: During this phase, the particles’ properties, such as mass and momentum, are mapped to the computational grid using interpolation functions.
elements.sent-2411-289: The mass at a grid node \( i \) is computed as: \begin{equation}m_i^n = \sum_p w_{ip}^n m_p,\end{equation} where \( m_p \) is the mass of particle \( p \), and \( w_{ip ^n \) is the interpolation weight (often derived from a B-spline kernel) between particle \( p \) and grid node \( i \).}
elements.sent-2411-290: The momentum at the grid node is similarly updated: \begin{equation}m_i^n v_i^n = \sum_p w_{ip}^n m_p \left( v_p^n + C_p^n (x_i - x_p^n) \right),\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{v _p^n \) is the velocity of particle \( p \), \( \mathbf{C _p^n \) represents the affine velocity field gradient, and \( \mathbf{x _i \) and \( \mathbf{x _p^n \) are the positions of the grid node and particle, respectively.. Grid Update.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}m_i^n v_i^n = \sum_p w_{ip}^n m_p \left( v_p^n + C_p^n (x_i - x_p^n) \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-291: Once particle properties are transferred, grid velocities are updated by accounting for external forces, internal stresses, and gravity.
elements.sent-2411-292: The velocity at grid node i is computed as: \begin{equation}v_i^{n+1} = v_i^n - \frac{\Delta t}{m_i^n} \sum_p \tau_p^n \nabla w_{ip}^n V_p^0 + \Delta t g,\end{equation} where \( \Delta t \) is the time step, \( \boldsymbol{\tau _p^n \) is the stress tensor of the particle \( p \), \( V_p^0 \) is the initial volume of the particle, and \( \mathbf{g \) is the acceleration due to gravity.. Grid-to-Particle (G2P) Transfer.}}

elements.sent-2411-293: After the grid is updated, the changes in velocity and momentum are transferred back to the particles.
elements.sent-2411-294: The particle velocity is updated using the grid velocities and interpolation weights: \begin{equation}v_p^{n+1} = \sum_i v_i^{n+1} w_{ip}^n,\end{equation} and the new position of the particle is given by: \begin{equation}x_p^{n+1} = x_p^n + \Delta t v_p^{n+1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-295: Additionally, the affine velocity field gradient \( \mathbf{C _p^{n+1 \) and deformation gradient \( \mathbf{F _p^{n+1 \) are updated as: \begin{equation}C_p^{n+1} = \frac{4}{(\Delta x)^2} \sum_i w_{ip}^n v_i^{n+1} (x_i - x_p^n)^T,\end{equation} \begin{equation}F_p^{n+1} = (I + \Delta t C_p^{n+1}) F_p^n.\end{equation}.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-311: In this paper, we propose \our, a physics-based method that efficiently transforms static 3D objects into interactive ones capable of responding to new interactions, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser .}
elements.sent-2411-312: Inspired by how humans predict physical properties of objects through visual data, \our leverages MLLM-based Physical Property Perception (MLLM-P3) to predict the mean values of physical properties.
elements.sent-2411-313: Unlike previous methods~\cite{zhang2024physdreamer,liu2024physics3d,huang2024dreamphysics iteratively refining physical properties through video analysis, we reformulate this problem to a probability distribution estimation task by predicting the full range of these properties based on the mean value and the object's geometry, reducing computational demands.}
elements.sent-2411-314: Finally, \our simulates object interactions in a 3D scene with driving particles sampled by the Physical-Geometric Adaptive Sampling (PGAS) strategy.
elements.sent-2411-315: We provide an overview of the comparison to previous works in Tab.~\ref{tab:comparison_concurrent .}
elements.sent-2411-316: In summary, \our makes the following contributions:.

elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig/pipeline.pdf} \caption{\textbf{Overview of \our \raisebox{-0.5\baselineskip}{\includegraphics[scale=0.025]{Fig/logo.pdf}}}.Given a pre-trained 3D scene and its corresponding 2D images, we first perform object-level segmentation of the 3D scene with the prior from a set of foundation meodels~\cite{liu2023grounding,kirillov2023segment,zhang2024recognize}. We obtain the mean physical properties of the object from the proposed MLLM-P3, and based on this and the object’s geometry, we then derive the full distribution using the MPDP model. Finally, we animate the 3D objects using a physics-based simulator with driving particles sampled via the Physical-Geometric Adaptive Sampling (PGAS) strategy.} \label{fig:pipeline}
